run_sim_env_data: Run the canvas simulation to see environment data.
route_sandbox_hint: Build a food web by hand.
title: Ecosystem Simulator
field_brief_lead: Pick a route, watch the live population system, and use the graph or campaign when you need evidence.
hero_explore_hint: Click any species to see what it eats and what eats it. Trophic-level cascades become obvious — remove a top predator and watch the system reorganize.
hero_sandbox_hint: Drop in producers, consumers, and predators; watch population dynamics emerge. Lotka-Volterra cycles appear when you have one predator + one prey + nothing else.
hero_conserve_hint: Explore a Maine-inspired 10-year teaching scenario. Population, habitat, and public-support values are 0-100 indices, and hand-authored cascade rules are not forecasts.
hero_inquiry_hint: Adjust predator birth, prey lifespan, and resource scarcity. Watch which discrete regime the system settles into. No score, no reveal, no answer dump — just slider sweep and observation.
hero_quiz_hint: Six questions cover predator-prey lag, model purpose, predation, carrying capacity, primary producers, and trophic levels.
hero_badges_hint: Achievements track which ecological concepts you have demonstrated, not just visited. Trophic-cascade badge requires you to actually trigger one in the sandbox.
explore_goal_body: compare settings that produce cycles, damping, or collapse in these teaching models. Predator peaks often lag prey peaks, but the amount of lag depends on the parameters. The animated canvas uses separate stochastic rules; the graph below uses a deterministic logistic predator-prey equation.
aria_live_pop_hist_pre: Live predator and prey population history over
canvas_generating_data: Canvas simulation is generating live data...
explore_model_desc: Explore a logistic extension of the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model. Adjust initial populations and coefficients to compare cycles, density dependence, and extinction in a discrete Euler approximation.
teaching_model_note: Teaching model: one-step Euler updates with rounded counts. It omits age structure, space, seasons, stochasticity, handling time, disease, and changing K; parameter values are illustrative rather than fitted to rabbits and foxes.
sandbox_goal_body: design experiments. Try the textbook Lotka-Volterra setup (1 predator + 1 prey, nothing else) and watch the cycle. Then break it: add a second predator (competitive exclusion), or remove the predator entirely (prey overshoots carrying capacity and crashes). Inject events to test resilience. Every entity placed is graded by Carrying Capacity (K) on the slider below.
aria_sandbox_prefix: Ecosystem sandbox. Click to place prey (left) or predators (right).
select_tool_intro: Select a tool above, then click on the canvas to interact:
instr_place: Place Rabbit/Fox/Tree: Click anywhere to spawn
instr_erase: Erase: Click near an entity to remove it
instr_move: Move: Click and drag an entity to reposition it
instr_tip: Tip: Pause the simulation first for precise placement!
exp_idea_1: Remove all predators and watch what happens to prey and vegetation
exp_idea_2: Create a "wall" of trees and see if it affects hunting patterns
exp_idea_3: Add many foxes at once, then trigger a Food Boom to save the rabbits
exp_idea_4: Pause, place prey in one corner and predators in the other, then resume
exp_idea_5: Trigger a Wildfire then immediately add trees to observe recovery
ai_no_text_fallback: The reader returned no text. Try again in a moment.
ai_offline: The AI reader is offline right now. Try again in a moment.
ai_unavailable: AI is not available in this context.
ai_reading_status: AI conservation biologist is reading your ecosystem...
ai_disclaimer2: It is not a Wabanaki person, not a wildlife professional, and does not speak for any Wabanaki nation, agency, or organization.
ai_disclaimer3: For authoritative voices on Maine conservation work, consult Penobscot Cultural and Historic Preservation Department, Passamaquoddy Cultural Heritage Museum, Wabanaki Public Health and Wellness, Maine Indian Basketmakers Alliance, Maine Department of Inland Fisheries and Wildlife, and the Atlantic Salmon Federation.
